splits.legalQuestion: Whether a multiemployer pension plan actuary may select actuarial assumptions for calculating withdrawal liability after the statutory measurement date (the end of the prior plan year), or whether all such assumptions must be fixed by that date.
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether ERISA requires actuarial assumptions used to calculate employer withdrawal liability to be locked in by the end of the prior plan year or whether actuaries may select assumptions after that date. The outcome affects billions of dollars in potential obligations for thousands of employers in multiemployer pension plans.

positions.label: Assumptions must be fixed by measurement date
positions.summary: Holds that all actuarial assumptions must be established by the statutory measurement date (end of the prior plan year), providing employers with greater predictability.

circuits.name: Seventh Circuit
circuits.caseName: M & K Employee Solutions v. Trustees of the IAM National Pension Fund

positions.label: Assumptions may be selected after measurement date
positions.summary: Holds that actuaries may select actuarial assumptions after the statutory measurement date, allowing plans to use more current data in calculating withdrawal liability.

circuits.name: Second Circuit
circuits.caseName: Trustees of the IAM National Pension Fund v. M & K Employee Solutions

splits.relatedScotusTitle: M & K Employee Solutions v. Trustees of the IAM National Pension Fund

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the term of supervised release is tolled (paused) when a supervisee absconds from supervision, thereby extending the period during which the person can be found to have violated the conditions of supervised release.
splits.description: Circuits split on whether federal courts can apply a judicially-created 'fugitive tolling' doctrine to supervised release, effectively extending court-ordered conditions beyond the time specified in the judgment when a person absconds. This affects sentencing calculations at revocation hearings for thousands of individuals who abscond from supervised release each year.

positions.label: Supervised release is tolled during absconding
positions.summary: Holds that the term of supervised release is tolled when a supervisee absconds, extending the court's authority to revoke supervised release beyond the original term.

positions.label: Supervised release is not tolled
positions.summary: Holds that there is no statutory authority for tolling supervised release when a supervisee absconds, so the term expires as originally set regardless of the supervisee's flight.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether Section 2244(b)(1)'s bar on relitigating previously raised claims in habeas proceedings applies to federal prisoners filing motions under Section 2255.
splits.description: Circuits are split 6-to-3 on whether federal prisoners face the categorical bar of § 2244(b)(1) — which prohibits raising any claim previously presented — when bringing successive collateral attacks under § 2255, in addition to § 2255(h)'s separate requirements. The outcome determines whether federal prisoners face a stricter gatekeeping requirement than Congress may have intended.

positions.label: Section 2244(b)(1) applies to § 2255 motions
positions.summary: Holds that the § 2244(b)(1) bar on previously raised claims applies to federal prisoners filing successive § 2255 motions, categorically barring any claim that was previously presented.

positions.label: Section 2244(b)(1) does not apply to § 2255 motions
positions.summary: Holds that § 2244(b)(1)'s bar does not apply to successive § 2255 motions by federal prisoners, who need only satisfy § 2255(h)'s requirements of newly discovered evidence or a new rule of constitutional law.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether a candidate for federal office has Article III standing to challenge a state law that allows the counting of mail-in ballots received after Election Day.
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether candidates have standing to challenge election rules pre-election based on competitive injury theories. The split affects whether pre-election challenges to voting procedures can proceed or must wait until post-election, with significant implications for election administration nationwide.

positions.label: Candidate has standing
positions.summary: Holds that a candidate suffers a cognizable competitive injury from election rules that allegedly dilute lawful votes, granting standing to challenge such rules pre-election.

circuits.name: Seventh Circuit
circuits.caseName: Bost v. Illinois State Board of Elections

positions.label: Candidate lacks standing
positions.summary: Holds that a candidate does not suffer a sufficiently concrete and particularized injury from the mere existence of an election rule permitting late ballot receipt, and thus lacks Article III standing.

splits.relatedScotusTitle: Bost v. Illinois State Board of Elections

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the rule of lenity takes priority over agency or Sentencing Commission deference (Auer/Kisor deference) when interpreting ambiguous provisions that carry criminal penalties or sentencing enhancements.
splits.description: Circuits are split on whether lenity trumps deference to agency interpretations in the criminal context. At least three circuits hold that lenity prevails over deference, while the Third Circuit has held that agency deference can trump lenity. The issue has become more salient after Loper Bright overruled Chevron deference.

positions.label: Lenity trumps deference
positions.summary: Holds that the rule of lenity takes precedence over Auer/Kisor or Chevron deference when interpreting ambiguous criminal provisions, preventing agencies from expanding criminal liability through interpretive guidance.

positions.label: Deference trumps lenity
positions.summary: Holds that agency or Sentencing Commission deference can override the rule of lenity when interpreting ambiguous criminal sentencing provisions, allowing anti-defendant readings to prevail.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the word 'and' in the First Step Act's safety-valve provision, 18 U.S.C. § 3553(f)(1), is conjunctive (requiring all three criminal-history criteria to disqualify a defendant) or disjunctive (any one criterion disqualifies).
splits.description: Circuits are deeply split on whether defendants are disqualified from safety-valve relief only if they meet all three criminal-history criteria listed in § 3553(f)(1), or whether possessing any one criterion is disqualifying. This affects whether district courts must impose sentences without regard to statutory minimums for many drug offenders.

positions.label: 'And' is conjunctive — all three criteria required to disqualify
positions.summary: Holds that defendants are disqualified from safety-valve relief only if they meet all three criminal-history criteria, reading 'and' in its natural conjunctive sense.

positions.label: 'And' is disjunctive or distributive — any one criterion disqualifies
positions.summary: Holds that possessing any one of the three criminal-history criteria disqualifies a defendant from safety-valve relief, reading 'and' as disjunctive or distributive in context.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether Application Note 1 to U.S.S.G. § 4B1.2, which includes conspiracy and other inchoate offenses within the definition of 'controlled substance offense' for career-offender purposes, is entitled to deference under Stinson/Kisor when the guideline text does not mention inchoate offenses.
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether the Sentencing Commission's commentary expanding 'controlled substance offense' to include conspiracy is a valid interpretation of an ambiguous guideline or an impermissible expansion beyond the guideline text. This split directly affects which defendants qualify for the career-offender enhancement and its significantly higher sentencing range.

positions.label: Commentary is entitled to deference — inchoate offenses included
positions.summary: Holds that Application Note 1 is a reasonable interpretation of an ambiguous guideline and is entitled to controlling weight, so conspiracy qualifies as a 'controlled substance offense' for career-offender purposes.

positions.label: Commentary exceeds guideline text — inchoate offenses excluded
positions.summary: Holds that the guideline text unambiguously does not include inchoate offenses, so the commentary impermissibly expands the definition and is not entitled to deference after Kisor.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether a non-retroactive change in sentencing law, such as the First Step Act's anti-stacking amendment to 18 U.S.C. § 924(c), can constitute or contribute to 'extraordinary and compelling reasons' for compassionate release under 18 U.S.C. § 3582(c)(1)(A).
splits.description: Circuits are split on whether non-retroactive changes in law can be considered as part of the 'extraordinary and compelling reasons' analysis for compassionate release. Some circuits categorically bar reliance on such changes, while others allow them as one factor. The Sentencing Commission's 2024 amendment to § 1B1.13(b)(6) attempted to resolve this but has itself become a source of disagreement about the Commission's authority.

positions.label: Non-retroactive changes may contribute to extraordinary and compelling reasons
positions.summary: Holds that non-retroactive changes in sentencing law may be considered as one factor among others in evaluating compassionate release motions, consistent with the Sentencing Commission's 2024 policy statement.

positions.label: Non-retroactive changes cannot contribute to extraordinary and compelling reasons
positions.summary: Holds that non-retroactive changes in law, whether alone or combined with other factors, cannot constitute extraordinary and compelling reasons for compassionate release because this would circumvent Congress's decision not to make the changes retroactive.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether a state drug conviction qualifies as a 'controlled substance offense' under the Sentencing Guidelines when the state schedule includes substances not listed on the federal Controlled Substances Act schedules.
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether the Guidelines' definition of 'controlled substance offense' in § 4B1.2(b) requires a match with federally scheduled substances or encompasses any offense under state law regardless of whether the substance is federally controlled. This affects career-offender and other sentencing enhancements for defendants with state drug convictions.

positions.label: State offense qualifies regardless of federal scheduling
positions.summary: Holds that the Guidelines broadly define 'controlled substance offense' to include any offense under state law, with no textual basis to graft a federal scheduling limitation onto the guideline.

positions.label: State offense must involve a federally scheduled substance
positions.summary: Holds that a state drug conviction qualifies as a 'controlled substance offense' only if it involves a substance that is also controlled under federal law, requiring a categorical match.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether federal prisoners challenging the conditions or terms of their confinement (as opposed to the fact or duration of confinement) must proceed under habeas corpus or may bring civil rights/Bivens actions.
splits.description: A longstanding circuit split exists on whether habeas corpus is the exclusive remedy for federal prisoners challenging conditions that affect the duration of confinement, such as BOP credit calculations and First Step Act time-credit decisions, or whether prisoners may also proceed through civil rights actions. This affects thousands of federal prisoners seeking to challenge BOP decisions.

positions.label: Habeas is exclusive remedy for duration-related challenges
positions.summary: Holds that when a prisoner's challenge to conditions of confinement effectively challenges the fact or duration of imprisonment, habeas corpus is the exclusive federal remedy.

positions.label: Civil rights actions are available alongside habeas
positions.summary: Holds that prisoners may bring civil rights or Bivens actions to challenge conditions of confinement even when those conditions relate to the terms of imprisonment, and habeas is not the exclusive remedy.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the civil or criminal appeal deadline applies to a district court's denial of a motion for attorney's fees under the Hyde Amendment.
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether a Hyde Amendment motion for attorney's fees in a criminal case is governed by the 30-day civil appeal deadline or the 14-day criminal appeal deadline. The Seventh Circuit joined the majority view that the civil deadline applies because the fee motion is a civil matter ancillary to the criminal case.

positions.label: Civil deadline applies
positions.summary: Holds that a Hyde Amendment motion for attorney's fees is a civil matter ancillary to the criminal case, so the 30-day civil appeal deadline under Fed. R. App. P. 4(a) applies.

positions.label: Criminal deadline applies
positions.summary: Holds that a Hyde Amendment motion arises in a criminal case, so the shorter 14-day criminal appeal deadline under Fed. R. App. P. 4(b) governs.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether post-petition, pre-conversion increases in the equity of a debtor's asset belong to the bankruptcy estate or to the debtor when a Chapter 13 case is converted in good faith to Chapter 7.
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether § 348(f)(1)(A) of the Bankruptcy Code means that appreciation in property value between the filing of a Chapter 13 petition and conversion to Chapter 7 inures to the estate or remains with the debtor. This affects debtors' incentive to convert and the distribution of assets to creditors.

positions.label: Appreciation belongs to the estate
positions.summary: Holds that the plain language of § 348(f)(1)(A), read with § 541(a)'s broad definition of estate property, means that post-petition appreciation inures to the bankruptcy estate upon conversion.

positions.label: Appreciation belongs to the debtor
positions.summary: Holds that the Bankruptcy Code's structure and legislative history indicate that post-petition, pre-conversion appreciation belongs to good-faith converting debtors rather than the estate.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether a district court may expressly impose a sentence upon revocation of supervised release for the purpose of punishing the defendant for the violation, or whether revocation sentences must be limited to sanctioning the breach of trust.
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether revocation sentences under 18 U.S.C. § 3583(e) may include a punitive component. The Sixth Circuit allows courts expressly to punish defendants for violations of supervised release, while other circuits limit the permissible purposes of revocation sentences to addressing the breach of trust.

positions.label: Punitive purpose permitted
positions.summary: Holds that district courts may revoke supervised release and impose sentences that expressly punish defendants for violations of supervised release conditions.

positions.label: Punitive purpose not permitted — breach of trust only
positions.summary: Holds that revocation sentences should address the defendant's breach of trust in the court's supervisory relationship, not serve as punishment for the underlying violation conduct.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether an order denying a motion to seal court documents containing private medical information is immediately appealable under the collateral order doctrine.
splits.description: The D.C. Circuit held that orders denying motions to seal medical information are immediately appealable as collateral orders because medical privacy is a value of 'high order' that would be effectively unreviewable after final judgment. Other circuits have not uniformly recognized this category of collateral orders, creating potential disagreement on the scope of the collateral order doctrine for privacy-related sealing motions.

positions.label: Immediately appealable as collateral order
positions.summary: Holds that denial of a motion to seal medical records is immediately appealable under the collateral order doctrine because medical privacy is a value of high order that would be lost if review is delayed until final judgment.

positions.label: Not immediately appealable
positions.summary: Holds that sealing orders generally do not satisfy the collateral order doctrine's requirements because adequate alternative remedies exist, such as protective orders or post-judgment appeal.

circuits.name: Second Circuit
circuits.caseName: In re City of New York

splits.legalQuestion: What legal standards govern claims of discrimination or unfair treatment in the operation of hospitality or business establishments under 42 U.S.C. § 1981 or similar federal law?
splits.description: The Supreme Court granted certiorari to review the Second Circuit's approach to legal standards for discrimination claims in hospitality settings, suggesting a circuit split on the applicable standards for liability and damages in such cases. The decision may resolve disagreements over proof requirements and available remedies.

positions.label: Second Circuit approach
positions.summary: Applied standards that the Supreme Court found sufficiently important or unsettled to warrant review in the context of discrimination claims against hospitality businesses.

positions.label: Other circuits' approach
positions.summary: Applied different standards for evaluating discrimination claims in business establishment contexts, contributing to the circuit split that prompted SCOTUS review.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the Federal Aviation Administration Authorization Act of 1994 (FAAAA) preempts state-law negligence claims against freight brokers who arrange transportation of property by motor carriers.
splits.description: The circuits are split on whether FAAAA's broad preemption clause, which prohibits states from enacting laws 'related to a price, route, or service of any motor carrier... with respect to the transportation of property,' extends to preempt state-law negligence claims against freight brokers for negligently selecting or supervising motor carriers. This affects liability exposure across the trucking industry.

positions.label: FAAAA preempts state negligence claims against brokers
positions.summary: Holds that state-law negligence claims against freight brokers are 'related to' the broker's services with respect to the transportation of property and are therefore preempted by the FAAAA.

circuits.name: Ninth Circuit
circuits.caseName: Miller v. C.H. Robinson Worldwide, Inc.

positions.label: FAAAA does not preempt state negligence claims against brokers
positions.summary: Holds that state-law negligence claims against freight brokers for negligent hiring or supervision of carriers are not preempted because they do not relate to the broker's 'prices, routes, or services.'

circuits.name: Eleventh Circuit
circuits.caseName: Montgomery v. Caribe Transport II, LLC

splits.relatedScotusTitle: Montgomery v. Caribe Transport II, LLC

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the Federal Insecticide, Fungicide, and Rodenticide Act (FIFRA) preempts state-law failure-to-warn claims based on allegedly inadequate product labeling when the EPA has not required the specific warning.
splits.description: Circuits are split on the scope of FIFRA's preemption of state failure-to-warn claims. The question is whether FIFRA's provision that states cannot impose labeling requirements 'in addition to or different from' federal requirements bars state tort claims that effectively require additional label warnings the EPA has not mandated.

positions.label: FIFRA preempts state failure-to-warn claims
positions.summary: Holds that FIFRA preempts state-law failure-to-warn claims that would require labeling changes beyond what the EPA has mandated, because such claims impose requirements 'in addition to or different from' federal labeling requirements.

positions.label: FIFRA does not preempt state failure-to-warn claims
positions.summary: Holds that state-law failure-to-warn claims survive FIFRA preemption when they are parallel to or consistent with FIFRA's misbranding provisions, because FIFRA does not occupy the entire field of pesticide labeling claims.

splits.relatedScotusTitle: Monsanto Company v. John L. Durnell

splits.legalQuestion: Whether a district court may consider factors related to potential errors or unfairness in a defendant's conviction or sentence as part of the 'extraordinary and compelling reasons' analysis for compassionate release under 18 U.S.C. § 3582(c)(1)(A).
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether claims that overlap with habeas corpus grounds — such as alleged errors or unfairness in the original conviction or sentence — may be considered in compassionate release motions. Some circuits allow such considerations as one factor, while others hold that § 3582(c)(1)(A) cannot be used as a substitute for § 2255 habeas relief.

positions.label: May consider conviction/sentence errors
positions.summary: Holds that district courts may consider claimed legal errors or unfairness in the original conviction or sentence as part of the extraordinary and compelling reasons analysis for compassionate release.

positions.label: Cannot use compassionate release for habeas-type claims
positions.summary: Holds that compassionate release under § 3582(c)(1)(A) is not a vehicle for relitigating the validity of a conviction or sentence, and such claims must be brought under § 2255.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the Religious Land Use and Institutionalized Persons Act (RLUIPA) authorizes individual-capacity damages suits against state prison officials.
splits.description: Circuits are split on whether RLUIPA creates a cause of action for money damages against state prison officials in their individual capacities. Some circuits have held that RLUIPA, enacted under the Spending Clause, does not clearly authorize personal-liability damages against individuals who are not direct recipients of federal funds, while others have allowed such claims to proceed.

positions.label: RLUIPA authorizes individual-capacity damages
positions.summary: Holds that RLUIPA provides a cause of action for money damages against state prison officials sued in their individual capacities for violations of prisoners' religious exercise rights.

positions.label: RLUIPA does not authorize individual-capacity damages
positions.summary: Holds that RLUIPA does not clearly authorize individual-capacity damages suits against state prison officials because Congress cannot impose personal liability on non-recipient individuals through the Spending Clause.

circuits.name: Fifth Circuit
circuits.caseName: Sossamon v. Lone Star State of Texas

splits.relatedScotusTitle: Landor v. Louisiana Department of Corrections and Public Safety

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the federal law prohibiting unlawful users of controlled substances from possessing firearms, 18 U.S.C. § 922(g)(3), violates the Second Amendment as applied to marijuana users.
splits.description: Following the Supreme Court's decision in Bruen establishing a historical-tradition test for Second Amendment challenges, the circuits have split on whether § 922(g)(3)'s prohibition on firearm possession by users of controlled substances (particularly marijuana) is constitutional. This implicates the intersection of expanding state marijuana legalization with federal firearms law.

positions.label: Section 922(g)(3) is constitutional
positions.summary: Holds that § 922(g)(3) does not violate the Second Amendment because historical tradition supports disarming individuals whose drug use renders them dangerous or untrustworthy with firearms.

positions.label: Section 922(g)(3) is unconstitutional as applied
positions.summary: Holds that § 922(g)(3) violates the Second Amendment as applied to marijuana users because the government has not demonstrated a sufficient historical tradition of disarming persons for drug use alone.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether workers who distribute baked goods to retail stores qualify as 'transportation workers engaged in foreign or interstate commerce' under Section 1 of the Federal Arbitration Act, thereby exempting them from mandatory arbitration agreements.
splits.description: Circuits disagree on how broadly to read the Section 1 exemption for transportation workers, particularly for last-mile delivery workers who transport goods within a single state as part of an interstate chain of commerce. This affects whether such workers can be compelled to arbitrate employment disputes.

positions.label: Broad reading — last-mile workers exempt
positions.summary: Holds that workers who are the last link in an interstate chain of commerce, even if operating within a single state, qualify as transportation workers engaged in interstate commerce and are exempt from the FAA.

circuits.name: First Circuit
circuits.caseName: Fraga v. Premium Retail Services, Inc.

positions.label: Narrow reading — last-mile workers not exempt
positions.summary: Holds that workers who deliver goods locally within a state do not qualify as transportation workers engaged in interstate commerce merely because the goods previously moved in interstate commerce.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether Section 47(b) of the Investment Company Act creates a private right of action allowing parties to a contract that violates the ICA to sue in federal court for rescission of that contract.
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether § 47(b) of the ICA provides an implied private right of action for rescission of contracts made in violation of the Act. This affects the ability of private parties to enforce the ICA's protections against abusive investment company practices through direct federal court actions.

positions.label: Section 47(b) creates a private right of action
positions.summary: Holds that § 47(b) creates an implied private right of action for rescission of contracts that violate the Investment Company Act, allowing private parties to sue directly.

circuits.name: Second Circuit
circuits.caseName: FS Credit Opportunities Corp. v. Saba Capital Master Fund

positions.label: Section 47(b) does not create a private right of action
positions.summary: Holds that § 47(b) is a voidability provision rather than a private right of action, and only the SEC can enforce the ICA's prohibitions.

splits.relatedScotusTitle: FS Credit Opportunities Corp. v. Saba Capital Master Fund

splits.legalQuestion: Whether Section 2 of the Voting Rights Act, as construed under the Gingles framework, can constitutionally compel race-based redistricting as a remedy for vote dilution, and whether compliance with Section 2 constitutes a compelling governmental interest justifying the use of race in drawing district lines.
splits.description: This case raises the question of whether race-conscious redistricting mandated by Section 2 of the Voting Rights Act is constitutionally permissible under the Equal Protection Clause. Lower courts have applied the Gingles framework to require consideration of race, but the constitutionality of this framework as applied is increasingly contested.

positions.label: Section 2 compels constitutionally permissible race-conscious redistricting
positions.summary: Holds that compliance with Section 2 of the VRA, as interpreted under the Gingles framework, constitutes a compelling interest that justifies the use of race in drawing district lines.

positions.label: Section 2's compelled race-based redistricting raises constitutional concerns
positions.summary: Holds or suggests that the Gingles framework's requirement of race-based redistricting may exceed constitutional limits under the Equal Protection Clause, particularly after Dobbs-era equal protection jurisprudence.

circuits.name: Eighth Circuit
circuits.caseName: Arkansas State Conference of the NAACP v. Arkansas Board of Apportionment
